From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Ammunition feeding device of a firearm

Not to be confused with [Magazine (artillery)](/wiki/Magazine_(artillery) "Magazine (artillery)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/9mm_pistol_magazine.jpg/250px-9mm_pistol_magazine.jpg)](/wiki/File:9mm_pistol_magazine.jpg)

A staggered-column [9×19mm](/wiki/9%C3%9719mm_Parabellum "9×19mm Parabellum") [Browning Hi-Power](/wiki/Browning_Hi-Power "Browning Hi-Power") pistol [box magazine](#Box). The top image shows the magazine loaded and ready for use, while the lower image shows it unloaded and disassembled.



Loading of small arms ammunition into a magazine.

A **magazine**, often simply called a **mag**, is an [ammunition](/wiki/Ammunition "Ammunition") storage and feeding device for a [repeating firearm](/wiki/Repeating_firearm "Repeating firearm"), either integral with the gun (internal or fixed external) or detachable. The magazine functions by holding several [cartridges](/wiki/Cartridge_(firearms) "Cartridge (firearms)") within itself and sequentially pushing each one into a position where it may be readily loaded into the [barrel](/wiki/Gun_barrel "Gun barrel") [chamber](/wiki/Chamber_(firearms) "Chamber (firearms)") by the firearm's moving [action](/wiki/Action_(firearms) "Action (firearms)"). The detachable magazine is sometimes colloquially referred to as a "[clip](/wiki/Clip_(firearms) "Clip (firearms)")," although this is technically inaccurate since a clip is actually an accessory device used to help load ammunition into the magazine or cylinder of a firearm.[[1]](#cite_note-nraila-firearms-glossary-1)[[2]](#cite_note-thegunzone-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Handgunnersglossary-3)

Magazines come in many different shapes and sizes from integral [tubular magazines](/wiki/Tubular_magazine "Tubular magazine") on [lever-action](/wiki/Lever-action "Lever-action") and [pump-action](/wiki/Pump-action "Pump-action") rifles and shotguns that may hold more than 5 rounds to detachable [box magazines](#Box) and [drum magazines](/wiki/Drum_magazine "Drum magazine") for [automatic rifles](/wiki/Automatic_rifle "Automatic rifle") and [light machine guns](/wiki/Light_machine_gun "Light machine gun") that may hold more than 50 rounds. Various jurisdictions ban what they define as "[high-capacity magazines](/wiki/High-capacity_magazine_ban "High-capacity magazine ban")".

## Nomenclature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Nomenclature")]

With the increased use of [semi-automatic](/wiki/Semi-automatic_firearm "Semi-automatic firearm") and [automatic firearms](/wiki/Automatic_firearm "Automatic firearm"), the detachable magazine became increasingly common. Soon after the adoption of the [M1911](/wiki/M1911 "M1911") pistol, the term "magazine" was settled on by the military and firearms experts. Although the term "[clip](/wiki/Clip_(firearms) "Clip (firearms)")" is often used to refer to detachable (never fixed) magazines, this usage remains a point of strong contention.[[4]](#cite_note-chauchat-4)[[5]](#cite_note-m1911-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) The defining difference between a clip and a magazine is the presence of a feed mechanism, typically a spring-loaded follower, which a clip lacks. Whereas a magazine consists of four parts: a spring, a spring follower, a body, and a base. Whereas a clip may be constructed out of a single continuous piece of stamped metal and may contain no moving parts. Examples of clips include *moon* clips for (e.g., [.38 Special](/wiki/.38_Special ".38 Special") and [.357 Magnum](/wiki/.357_Magnum ".357 Magnum")) [revolvers](/wiki/Revolver "Revolver"); *stripper* clips, as those used in association with [speedloaders](/wiki/Speedloader "Speedloader") for modern military (e.g., [5.56×45mm NATO](/wiki/5.56%C3%9745mm_NATO "5.56×45mm NATO") and [7.62x51mm NATO](/wiki/7.62x51mm_NATO "7.62x51mm NATO")) rifles; and *en bloc* clips for older [M1 Garand](/wiki/M1_Garand "M1 Garand") (e.g., [.30-06 Springfield](/wiki/.30-06_Springfield ".30-06 Springfield")) military rifles.[[2]](#cite_note-thegunzone-2)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[1]](#cite_note-nraila-firearms-glossary-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The earliest firearms were loaded with powder and a lead ball in paper, and to fire more than a single shot without reloading required multiple [barrels](/wiki/Gun_barrel "Gun barrel"), such as in [pepper-box](/wiki/Pepper-box "Pepper-box") guns, [double-barreled rifles](/wiki/Double_rifle "Double rifle"), [double-barreled shotguns](/wiki/Double-barreled_shotgun "Double-barreled shotgun"), or multiple [chambers](/wiki/Chamber_(firearms) "Chamber (firearms)"), such as in [revolvers](/wiki/Revolver "Revolver"). The main problem with these solutions is that they increase the bulk and/or weight of a firearm, over a firearm with a single barrel and/or single chamber. However, many attempts were made to get multiple shots from loading a single barrel through the use of [superposed loads](/wiki/Superposed_load "Superposed load").[[9]](#cite_note-sawyer3-9) While some early repeaters such as the [Kalthoff repeater](/wiki/Kalthoff_repeater "Kalthoff repeater") managed to operate using complex systems with multiple feed sources for ball, powder, and primer, easily mass-produced repeating mechanisms did not appear until self-contained cartridges were developed in the 19th century.

### Early tubular magazines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early tubular magazines")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Henry_Rifle_Loading_pos.JPG/250px-Henry_Rifle_Loading_pos.JPG)](/wiki/File:Henry_Rifle_Loading_pos.JPG)

Loading sleeve open, three Henry Flat cartridges, compare with .44 WCF round

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Spencer_rifle_diagram.png/250px-Spencer_rifle_diagram.png)](/wiki/File:Spencer_rifle_diagram.png)

Diagram of the Spencer rifle showing the tubular magazine in the butt

The first successful mass-produced repeating weapon to use a "tubular magazine" permanently mounted to the weapon was the Austrian Army's [Girandoni air rifle](/wiki/Girandoni_air_rifle "Girandoni air rifle"), first produced in 1779.

The first mass-produced repeating firearm was the [Volcanic Rifle](/wiki/Volcanic_Repeating_Arms "Volcanic Repeating Arms") which used a hollow bullet with the base filled with powder and primer fed into the chamber from a tube called a "magazine" with an integral spring to push the cartridges in to the action, thence to be loaded into the chamber and fired. It was named after a building or room used to store ammunition. The anemic power of the [Rocket Ball](/wiki/Rocket_Ball "Rocket Ball") ammunition used in the Volcanic doomed it to limited popularity.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

The [Henry repeating rifle](/wiki/Henry_repeating_rifle "Henry repeating rifle") is a [lever-action](/wiki/Lever-action "Lever-action"), [breech-loading](/wiki/Breech-loading "Breech-loading"), tubular magazine-fed [repeating rifle](/wiki/Repeating_rifle "Repeating rifle"), and was an improved version of the earlier Volcanic rifle. Designed by [Benjamin Tyler Henry](/wiki/Benjamin_Tyler_Henry "Benjamin Tyler Henry") in 1860, it was one of the first firearms to use self-contained [metallic cartridges](/wiki/Metallic_cartridge "Metallic cartridge"). The Henry was introduced in 1860 and was in production until 1866 in the United States by the [New Haven Arms Company](/wiki/New_Haven_Arms_Company "New Haven Arms Company"). It was adopted in small quantities by the [Union Army](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army") in the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") and was favored for its greater firepower than the standard issue [carbine](/wiki/Carbine "Carbine"). Many later found their way Westward and was famed both for its use at the [Battle of the Little Bighorn](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Little_Bighorn "Battle of the Little Bighorn"), and being the basis for the iconic [Winchester lever-action repeating rifle](/wiki/Winchester_rifle "Winchester rifle"), which is still in production to the present day.[[10]](#cite_note-navalenc-10) The [Henry](/wiki/Henry_rifle "Henry rifle") and [Winchester rifles](/wiki/Winchester_rifle "Winchester rifle") would go on to see service with a number of militaries including Turkey. Switzerland and Italy adopted similar designs.[[10]](#cite_note-navalenc-10)

The second magazine-fed firearm to achieve widespread success was the [Spencer repeating rifle](/wiki/Spencer_repeating_rifle "Spencer repeating rifle"), which was designed by [Christopher Miner Spencer](/wiki/Christopher_Miner_Spencer "Christopher Miner Spencer") in 1860, also saw military service during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). The Spencer used a tubular magazine located in the butt of the gun instead of under the barrel and it used [rimfire](/wiki/Rimfire_ammunition "Rimfire ammunition") metallic cartridges. The Spencer was successful, but the rimfire ammunition did occasionally ignite in the magazine tube and destroy the magazine. It could also injure or kill the user.

The new [bolt-action](/wiki/Bolt-action "Bolt-action") rifles began to gain favor with militaries in the mid-1880s and were often equipped with tubular magazines. The [Mauser Model 1871](/wiki/Mauser_Model_1871 "Mauser Model 1871") was originally a single-shot action that added a tubular magazine in its 1884 update. The Norwegian [Jarmann M1884](/wiki/Jarmann_M1884 "Jarmann M1884") was adopted in 1884 and also used a tubular magazine. The French [Lebel Model 1886 rifle](/wiki/Lebel_Model_1886_rifle "Lebel Model 1886 rifle") also used 8-round tubular magazine.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Tubular magazines remain in common use on many types of shotguns, including some rifles.

### Integral box magazines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Integral box magazines")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Rifles1905-2.jpg/250px-Rifles1905-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rifles1905-2.jpg)

1905 Military Rifles magazines.   
**1 & 2**: Mosin–Nagant M1891   
**3 & 4**: Lebel M1886   
**5 & 6**: Gewehr M1888   
**7 & 8**: Mannlicher M1888   
**9 & 10**: Lee–Metford M1888   
**11 & 12**: Dutch-Mannlicher M1895   
**13 & 14**: Mauser M1893   
**15**: Krag–Jørgensen M1886   
**16**: Schmidt–Rubin M1889

Two early [box magazine](#Box) patents were the ones by [Rollin White](/wiki/Rollin_White "Rollin White") in 1855 and William Harding in 1859.[[12]](#cite_note-12) A detachable box magazine was patented in 1864 by the American Robert Wilson. Unlike later box magazines this magazine fed into a tube magazine and was located in the stock of the gun.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) Another box magazine, closer to the modern type, though non-detachable, was patented in Britain (No. 483) by Mowbray Walker, George Henry Money and Francis Little in 1867.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[James Paris Lee](/wiki/James_Paris_Lee "James Paris Lee") patented a box magazine which held rounds stacked vertically in 1875,[[17]](#cite_note-17) 1879 and 1882, and was first adopted by Austria in the form of an 11mm straight-pull bolt-action rifle, the [Mannlicher M1886](/wiki/Mannlicher_M1886 "Mannlicher M1886"), possibly the first serially produced rifle with a box magazine. It also used a cartridge clip which held 5 rounds ready to load into the magazine.[[18]](#cite_note-eb1911-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The military cartridge was evolving as the magazine rifle evolved. Cartridges evolved from large-bore cartridges (.40 caliber/10 mm and larger) to smaller bores that fired lighter, higher-velocity bullets and incorporated new [smokeless propellants](/wiki/Smokeless_gunpowder "Smokeless gunpowder"). The [Lebel Model 1886 rifle](/wiki/Lebel_Model_1886_rifle "Lebel Model 1886 rifle") was the first rifle and cartridge to be designed for use with smokeless powder and used an 8 mm [wadcutter](/wiki/Wadcutter "Wadcutter")-shaped bullet that was drawn from a tubular magazine. This would later become a problem when the Lebel's ammunition was updated to use a more aerodynamic pointed bullet. Modifications had to be made to the [centerfire](/wiki/Centerfire "Centerfire") case to prevent the [spitzer](/wiki/Spitzer_(bullet) "Spitzer (bullet)") point from igniting the primer of the next cartridge inline in the magazine through recoil or simply rough handling.[[18]](#cite_note-eb1911-18) This remains a concern with lever-action firearms today.

The bolt-action [Krag–Jørgensen](/wiki/Krag%E2%80%93J%C3%B8rgensen "Krag–Jørgensen") rifle, designed in Norway in 1886, used a unique rotary magazine that was built into the receiver. Like Lee's box magazine, the rotary magazine held the rounds side-by-side, rather than end-to-end. Like most rotary magazines, it was loaded through a loading gate one round at a time, this one located on the side of the receiver. While reliable, the Krag–Jørgensen's magazine was expensive to produce and slow to reload. It was adopted by only three countries, Denmark in 1889, the United States in 1892,[[20]](#cite_note-krag-20) and Norway in 1894.

#### Clip-fed revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Clip-fed revolution")]

See also: [List of clip-fed firearms](/wiki/List_of_clip-fed_firearms "List of clip-fed firearms")

A clip (called a *charger* in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")) is a device that is used to store multiple rounds of ammunition together as a unit, ready for insertion into the magazine or cylinder of a firearm. This speeds up the process of reloading the firearm as several rounds can be loaded at once, rather than one round being loaded at a time. Several different types of clips exist, most of which are made of inexpensive metal stampings that are designed to be disposable, though they are often re-used.

The first clips used were of the *en bloc* variety, developed by [Ferdinand Mannlicher](/wiki/Ferdinand_Mannlicher "Ferdinand Mannlicher") for the Mannlicher M1886 rifle and first adopted by the [Austro-Hungarian Army](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Army "Austro-Hungarian Army"), which would be used during the first world war in the form of the [Mannlicher M1895](/wiki/Mannlicher_M1895 "Mannlicher M1895"), derivatives of which would be adopted by many national militaries. The Germans used this system for their [Model 1888 Commission Rifle](/wiki/Gewehr_1888 "Gewehr 1888"), featuring a 5-round *en bloc* clip-fed integral box magazine.[[21]](#cite_note-21) One problem with the *en bloc* system is that the firearm cannot be practically used without a ready supply of (mostly disposable) clips. [Paul](/wiki/Paul_Mauser "Paul Mauser") [Mauser](/wiki/Mauser "Mauser") would solve this problem by introducing a stripper clip that functioned only to assist the user in loading the magazine quickly: it was not required to load the magazine to full capacity. He would continue to make improved models of rifles that took advantage of this new clip design from [1889](/wiki/Mauser_Model_1889 "Mauser Model 1889") through [1898](/wiki/Gewehr_98 "Gewehr 98") in various calibers that proved enormously successful, and were adopted by a wide range of national militaries.[[18]](#cite_note-eb1911-18) In 1890 the French adopted the [8mm Lebel](/wiki/8_mm_Lebel "8 mm Lebel") [Berthier rifles](/wiki/Berthier_rifle "Berthier rifle") with 3-round integral magazines, fed from *en bloc* clips; the empty clips were pushed from the bottom of the action by the insertion of a loaded clip from the top.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-hawkslebel-23)

In the late 19th century, there were many short-lived designs, such as the [M1895 Lee Navy](/wiki/M1895_Lee_Navy "M1895 Lee Navy") and [Gewehr 1888](/wiki/Gewehr_1888 "Gewehr 1888"), eventually replaced by the [M1903 Springfield rifle](/wiki/M1903_Springfield_rifle "M1903 Springfield rifle") and [Gewehr 98](/wiki/Gewehr_98 "Gewehr 98") respectively. The [Russian](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") [Mosin–Nagant](/wiki/Mosin%E2%80%93Nagant "Mosin–Nagant"), adopted in 1891, was an exception. It was not revolutionary; it was a bolt-action rifle, used a small-bore smokeless powder cartridge, and a fixed box magazine loaded from the top with [stripper clips](/wiki/Stripper_clip "Stripper clip"), all of which were features that were used in earlier military rifles. What made the Nagant stand out was that it combined all the earlier features in a form that was to last virtually unchanged from its issue by Russia in 1894 through [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and with its sniper rifle variants still in use today.

#### Magazine cut-off

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Magazine cut-off")]

A feature of many late 19th and early 20th century bolt-action rifles was the magazine cut-off, sometimes called a feed interrupter. This was a mechanical device that prevented the rifle from loading a round from the magazine, requiring the shooter to manually load each individual round as he fired, saving the rounds in the magazine for short periods of rapid fire when ordered to use them. Most military authorities that specified them assumed that their riflemen would waste ammunition indiscriminately if allowed to load from the magazine all the time.[[24]](#cite_note-24) By the mid-20th century, most manufacturers deleted this feature to save costs and manufacturing time; it is also likely that battlefield experience had proven the futility of this philosophy.

#### Final fixed-magazine developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Final fixed-magazine developments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Clip_M1-SKS.JPG/250px-Clip_M1-SKS.JPG)](/wiki/File:Clip_M1-SKS.JPG)

Comparison of M1 Garand *en bloc* clip (left), and SKS stripper clip (right)

One of the last new clip-fed, fixed-magazine rifles widely adopted that was not a modification of an earlier rifle was the [M1 Garand](/wiki/M1_Garand "M1 Garand"). The M1 Garand was the first [gas-operated](/wiki/Gas-operated "Gas-operated") [semi-automatic rifle](/wiki/Semi-automatic_rifle "Semi-automatic rifle") adopted and issued in large numbers as the standard [service rifle](/wiki/Service_rifle "Service rifle") of any military in the world. The M1 Garand was fed by a special eight-round *en bloc* clip. The clip itself was inserted into the rifle's magazine during loading, where it was locked in place. The rounds were fed directly from the clip, with a spring-loaded follower in the rifle pushing the rounds up into feeding position. When empty, the bolt would lock open, and a spring would automatically eject the empty clip with a distinctive pinging sound, leaving the rifle ready to be quickly reloaded. The [M14 rifle](/wiki/M14_rifle "M14 rifle"), which was based on incremental changes to the Garand action, switched to a detachable box magazine.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, the M14 with magazine attached could also be loaded via 5-round stripper-clips.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

The Soviet [SKS](/wiki/SKS "SKS") carbine, which entered service in 1945, was something of a stopgap between the semi-automatic service rifles being developed in the period leading up to World War II, and the new [assault rifle](/wiki/Assault_rifle "Assault rifle") developed by the Germans. The SKS used a fixed magazine, holding ten rounds and fed by a conventional stripper clip. It was a modification of the earlier [AVS-36](/wiki/AVS-36 "AVS-36") rifle, shortened and chambered for the new reduced power [7.62×39mm](/wiki/7.62%C3%9739mm "7.62×39mm") cartridge. It was rendered obsolete for military use almost immediately by the 1947 introduction of the magazine-fed [AK-47](/wiki/AK-47 "AK-47") assault rifle, though it remained in service for many years in Soviet Bloc nations alongside the AK-47. The detachable magazine quickly came to dominate post-war military rifle designs.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Detachable box magazines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Detachable box magazines")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/M1-M14-M16-magazines.JPG/250px-M1-M14-M16-magazines.JPG)](/wiki/File:M1-M14-M16-magazines.JPG)

(left to right)   
[M1 Garand](/wiki/M1_Garand_rifle "M1 Garand rifle") 8-round [*en bloc* clip](/wiki/Clip_(firearms)#En_bloc "Clip (firearms)"),   
[M14](/wiki/M14_rifle "M14 rifle") 20-round magazine,   
[M16 STANAG](/wiki/STANAG_4179 "STANAG 4179") 20- and 30-round magazines

Firearms using detachable magazines are made with an opening known as a *magazine well* into which the detachable magazine is inserted. The magazine well locks the magazine in position for feeding cartridges into the chamber of the firearm, and requires a device known as a ***magazine release*** to allow the magazine to be separated from the firearm.[[26]](#cite_note-emb-26)

The [M1879 Remington-Lee](/wiki/M1885_Remington%E2%80%93Lee "M1885 Remington–Lee") was the first rifle with a successful detachable box magazine design. Its followup, the [Lee–Metford](/wiki/Lee%E2%80%93Metford "Lee–Metford") rifle, developed in 1888, also has a detachable box magazine, and the spare one could be optionally worn on [soldier equipment](/wiki/Slade%E2%80%93Wallace_equipment "Slade–Wallace equipment"),[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) although with the adoption of the [Short Magazine Lee–Enfield Mk I](/wiki/Lee%E2%80%93Enfield#Short_Magazine_Lee–Enfield_Mk_I "Lee–Enfield") this became only detachable for cleaning and not swapped to reload the weapon.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The first completely modern removable box magazine was patented in 1908 by [Arthur Savage](/wiki/Arthur_William_Savage "Arthur William Savage") for the [Savage Model 99](/wiki/Savage_Model_99 "Savage Model 99") (1899),[[33]](#cite_note-33) although it was not implemented on the 99 until 1965.[[34]](#cite_note-34) James Paris Lee's patent of November 4, 1879, Number 221,328 would have been before Arthur Savage's magazine.

Lee's magazine was also used on the Remington Lee model 1899 factory sporting rifle. Other guns did not adopt all of its features until his patent expired in 1942: It has shoulders to retain cartridges when it is removed from the rifle. It operates reliably with cartridges of different lengths. It is insertable and removable at any time with any number of cartridges. These features allow the operator to reload the gun infrequently, carry magazines rather than loose cartridges, and to easily change the types of cartridges in the field. The magazine is assembled from inexpensive stamped sheet metal. It also includes a crucial safety feature for hunting dangerous game: when empty the follower[[35]](#cite_note-35) stops the bolt from engaging the chamber, informing the operator that the gun is empty before any attempt to fire.

The first successful [semi-automatic pistol](/wiki/Semi-automatic_pistol "Semi-automatic pistol") was the [Borchardt C-93](/wiki/Borchardt_C-93 "Borchardt C-93") (1893) and incorporated detachable box magazines. Nearly all subsequent semiautomatic pistol designs adopted detachable box magazines.[[26]](#cite_note-emb-26)

The Swiss Army evaluated the [Luger pistol](/wiki/Luger_pistol "Luger pistol") using a detachable box magazine in [7.65×21mm Parabellum](/wiki/7.65%C3%9721mm_Parabellum "7.65×21mm Parabellum") and adopted it in 1900 as its standard sidearm. The Luger pistol was accepted by the Imperial German Navy in 1904. This version is known as Pistole 04 (or P.04). In 1908 the German Army adopted the Luger to replace the Reichsrevolver in front-line service. The Pistole 08 (or P.08) was chambered in [9×19mm Parabellum](/wiki/9%C3%9719mm_Parabellum "9×19mm Parabellum"). The P.08 was the usual side arm for German Army personnel in both [World Wars](/wiki/World_Wars "World Wars").

The [M1911 semi-automatic pistol](/wiki/M1911_pistol "M1911 pistol") set the standard for most modern handguns and likewise the mechanics of the handgun magazine. In most handguns the magazine follower engages a slide-stop to hold the slide back and keep the firearm out of battery when the magazine is empty and all rounds fired. Upon inserting a loaded magazine, the user depresses the slide stop, throwing the slide forward, stripping a round from the top of the magazine stack and chambering it. In single-action pistols this action keeps the hammer cocked back as the new round is chambered, keeping the gun ready to begin firing again.

During World War One, detachable box magazines found favor, being used in all manner of firearms, such as pistols, light-machine guns, submachine guns, semi-automatic and automatic rifles. However, after the [War to End All Wars](/wiki/War_to_End_All_Wars "War to End All Wars"), military planners failed to recognize the importance of automatic rifles and detachable box magazine concept, and instead maintained their traditional views and preference for clip-fed [bolt-action rifles](/wiki/Bolt-action_rifle "Bolt-action rifle"). As a result, many promising new automatic rifle designs that used detachable box magazines were abandoned.

As World War II loomed, most of the world's major powers began to develop submachine guns fed by 20- to 40-round detachable box magazines. However, of the major powers, only the United States would adopt a general-issue semi-automatic rifle that used detachable box magazines: the [M1 carbine](/wiki/M1_carbine "M1 carbine") with its 15-round magazines. As the war progressed the Germans developed the [Sturmgewehr 44](/wiki/Sturmgewehr_44 "Sturmgewehr 44") assault rifle concept with its 30-round detachable magazine. After WWII, automatic weapons using detachable box magazines were developed and used by all of the world's armies. Today, detachable box magazines are the norm and they are so widely used that they are simply referred to as magazines or "mags" for short.

### Double-stack magazines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Double-stack magazines")]

The earliest examples of double-stack, single-feed box magazine patents are by the Austro-Hungarian Karl Krnka in July 1888,[[36]](#cite_note-36) and the English inventor Joseph James Speed of Waltham Cross in November 1888.[[37]](#cite_note-37) The earliest double-feed examples are by the British George Vincent Fosbery in 1883 and 1884,[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) and Fritz von Stepski and Erich Sterzinger of Austria-Hungary in November 1888.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

One of the earliest rifles with a double-stack staggered-feed magazine is the 1889 [Schmidt-Rubin](/wiki/Schmidt-Rubin "Schmidt-Rubin"). James Paris Lee is sometimes claimed to have invented the double-stack, staggered-feed detachable box magazine but he did not design one until 1892 for the Mark II [Lee-Metford](/wiki/Lee-Metford "Lee-Metford"), three years after the Schmidt-Rubin.

The first pistol with a double-stack, staggered-feed magazine was the [Mauser C96](/wiki/Mauser_C96 "Mauser C96") although it was an integral design fed by stripper clips and located in front of the grip rather than in the modern configuration of in the grip. A detachable, double-stack staggered-feed box magazine was used in the Bergmann Mars pistol, designed in 1903, but it too was in front of the grip rather than in it.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The first detachable double-stack, single-feed magazine in the grip for pistols was probably the one patented by the American Elbert H. Searle in 1904 and adopted by [Arthur Savage](/wiki/Arthur_William_Savage "Arthur William Savage") though he did not apply it in practice to his designs until much later.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

During World War One, Schmeisser's Cone was invented in 1916 by [Hugo Schmeisser](/wiki/Hugo_Schmeisser "Hugo Schmeisser") which allowed high-capacity double-stack, single-feed box magazine using guns to function reliably although it wasn't implemented on any of his designs until after the war. The first reliable high-capacity double-stack, staggered-feed box magazine was developed by the American designer Oscar V. Payne for the [Thompson submachine gun](/wiki/Thompson_submachine_gun "Thompson submachine gun") around the same time as Schmeisser's Cone.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

## Function and types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Function and types")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/SKS_magazine.JPG/250px-SKS_magazine.JPG)](/wiki/File:SKS_magazine.JPG)

[SKS](/wiki/SKS "SKS") integral box magazine

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Magasin_d%27un_F_ass_90.jpg/250px-Magasin_d%27un_F_ass_90.jpg)](/wiki/File:Magasin_d%27un_F_ass_90.jpg)

Detachable box magazine for a [SIG SG 550](/wiki/SIG_SG_550 "SIG SG 550") with studs for stacking multiple magazines together.

All cartridge-based single-barrel firearms designed to fire more than a single round of ammunition without manual reloading require some form of magazine designed to store and feed cartridges into the firearm's action. Magazines come in many shapes and sizes, with the most common type in modern firearms being the detachable box type. Most magazines designed for use with a [reciprocating](/wiki/Reciprocating_motion "Reciprocating motion") bolt firearm (tube fed firearms being the exception) make use of a set of feed lips which stop the vertical motion of the cartridges out of the magazine but allow one cartridge at a time to be pushed forward (stripped) out of the feed lips by the firearm's bolt into the chamber. Some form of spring and follower combination is almost always used to feed cartridges to the lips which can be located either in the magazine (most removable box magazines) or built into the firearm (fixed box magazines). There are also two distinct styles to feed lips. In a single-feed design the top cartridge touches both lips and is commonly used in single-column box magazines, while a staggered feed magazine (sometimes called "double-feed" magazine, not to be confused with the [firearm malfunction](/wiki/Firearm_malfunction#Double_feed_(Type_3_Malfunction) "Firearm malfunction")) consists of a wider set of lips so that the second cartridge in line forces the top cartridge against one of the lips. The staggered-feed design has proven more resistant to jamming in use with double-column magazines than single-feed variants,[[45]](#cite_note-45) since the narrowing of a magazine tube to a single-feed induces extra friction which the magazine springs needs to overcome.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Some magazine types are strongly associated with certain firearm types, such as the fixed "tubular" magazine found on most modern [lever-action](/wiki/Lever-action "Lever-action") rifles and [pump-action](/wiki/Pump_action "Pump action") shotguns. A firearm using detachable magazines may accept a variety of types of magazine, such as the [Thompson submachine gun](/wiki/Thompson_submachine_gun "Thompson submachine gun"), most variations of which would accept box or drum magazines. Some types of firearm, such as the [M249](/wiki/M249_light_machine_gun "M249 light machine gun") and other [squad automatic weapons](/wiki/Squad_automatic_weapon "Squad automatic weapon"), can feed from both magazines and belts.

### Tubular

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Tubular")]

Many of the first [repeating rifles](/wiki/Repeating_rifle "Repeating rifle") and [shotguns](/wiki/Shotgun "Shotgun"), particularly [lever-action](/wiki/Lever-action "Lever-action") rifles and [pump-action](/wiki/Pump-action "Pump-action") shotguns, used magazines that stored cartridges nose-to-end inside of a spring-loaded tube that typically runs parallel underneath the barrel, or inside of the buttstock. Tubular magazines are also commonly used in [.22 caliber](/wiki/.22_caliber ".22 caliber") [bolt-action](/wiki/Bolt-action "Bolt-action") [rimfire](/wiki/Rimfire_ammunition "Rimfire ammunition") rifles, such as the [Marlin Model XT](/wiki/Marlin_Model_XT-22 "Marlin Model XT-22"). Tubular magazines and centerfire cartridges with pointed ([spitzer](/wiki/Spitzer_(bullet) "Spitzer (bullet)")) bullets present a safety issue: a pointed bullet may (through the forces of recoil or simply rough handling) strike the next round's [primer](/wiki/Primer_(firearm) "Primer (firearm)") and ignite that round, or even cause a chain ignition of other rounds, within the magazine. The [Winchester Model 1873](/wiki/Winchester_rifle#Model_1873 "Winchester rifle") used blunt-nosed centerfire cartridges such as the [.44-40 Winchester](/wiki/.44-40_Winchester ".44-40 Winchester"). Certain modern rifle cartridges using soft pointed plastic tips have been designed to avoid this problem while improving the aerodynamic qualities of the bullet to match those available in bolt-action designs, therefore extending the effective range of lever-actions.

### Box

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Box")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Magazine_Types_Single_Stack_Double_Stack_Double_Feed_CC_BY-SA_4.0_by_Grasyl.svg/250px-Magazine_Types_Single_Stack_Double_Stack_Double_Feed_CC_BY-SA_4.0_by_Grasyl.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Magazine_Types_Single_Stack_Double_Stack_Double_Feed_CC_BY-SA_4.0_by_Grasyl.svg)

Left and centre: single-column, single-feed (aka *single-stack, single-feed*), and double-column, single feed (aka *staggered-stack, single-feed*) detachable box magazines. Right: double-column, double-feed (aka *staggered-stack, staggered-feed*) design.

The most popular type of magazine in modern rifles and handguns, box magazines, arrange the cartridges in columns, either in a single-stack or in a double-stack or staggered ([zigzag](/wiki/Zigzag "Zigzag")) fashion. In the latter case, the magazine is often referred to as *double-column* or *double-stack* that allows for greatly increased capacity compared to *single-column* or *single-stack* designs. As the firearm cycles, a spring-loaded follower drives the cartridges toward the end of the magazine, ultimately terminating in either a single position (center-feed) or alternating between left and right positions (staggered-feed). Box magazines may be integral to the firearm or removable:

* An *integral box* or *fixed box* magazine (also known as a *blind box* magazine when lacking a removable floorplate) is built into the firearm and is not easily removable. This type of magazine is found most often on [bolt-action](/wiki/Bolt-action "Bolt-action") rifles. An integral box magazine is usually charged through the action, one round at a time. Military rifles often use stripper clips, a.k.a. chargers, permitting multiple rounds, commonly with 5 or 10 at a time, to be loaded in rapid sequence. Some integral box magazines use *en bloc* clips that are loaded into the magazine with the ammunition and that are ejected from the firearm when empty.
* A *detachable box* magazine is a self-contained mechanism capable of being loaded or unloaded while detached from the host firearm. They are attached via a slot in the firearm receiver, usually below the action, to the side of the action, or on top of the action. When necessary, the magazine can easily be detached from the firearm and replaced by another. This significantly speeds the process of reloading, allowing the operator quick access to ammunition. This type of magazine may be straight or curved, the curve being necessary if the rifle uses rimmed ammunition or ammunition with a tapered case. Detachable box magazines may be metal or plastic. The plastic magazines are sometimes partially transparent so the operator can easily check the remaining ammunition. Box magazines are often affixed to each other with clamps, clips, tape, straps, or built-in studs to facilitate faster reloading: see [jungle style](/wiki/Jungle_style "Jungle style").

There are, however, exceptions to these rules. The [Lee–Enfield](/wiki/Lee%E2%80%93Enfield "Lee–Enfield") rifle had a detachable box magazine only to facilitate cleaning. The Lee–Enfield magazine did open, permitting rapid unloading of the magazine without having to operate the bolt-action repeatedly to unload the magazine. Other designs, like the [Breda Modello 30](/wiki/Breda_30 "Breda 30"), had a fixed protruding magazine from the right side that resembled a conventional detachable box, but it was non-detachable and only reloaded by using 20 round [stripper clips](/wiki/Stripper_clip "Stripper clip").

Box magazines may come in straight, angled, or curved forms depending if the cartridges are [tapered](/wiki/Tapering_(firearms) "Tapering (firearms)") [rimmed/rimless](/wiki/Rim_(firearms) "Rim (firearms)") or bottlenecked. Straight or slightly curved magazines work well with straight-sided rimless cartridges, angled magazines work well with straight-sided rimmed or rimless cartridges, and curved magazines work well with rimmed/rimless tapered cartridges.

Pistol magazines are most often found with single-stack or double-stack, single-feed, which may be due to this design being slimmer at the top, which can simplify the design of the pistol frame inregard to its grip thickness.

#### Horizontal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Horizontal")]

[![Photo of a loaded FN P90 magazine](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/FNP90MAG02.jpg/120px-FNP90MAG02.jpg)](/wiki/File:FNP90MAG02.jpg)

[![Photo of a partially loaded FN P90 magazine](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/FNP90MAG01.JPG/250px-FNP90MAG01.JPG)](/wiki/File:FNP90MAG01.JPG)

The P90's magazine has a capacity of 50 rounds, and it fits flush with the weapon's frame.[[47]](#cite_note-dockery2007-47)

The [FN P90](/wiki/FN_P90 "FN P90"), [Kel-Tec P50](/wiki/Kel-Tec_P50 "Kel-Tec P50"), and [AR-57](/wiki/AR-57 "AR-57") [personal defense weapons](/wiki/Personal_defense_weapon "Personal defense weapon") use horizontally mounted feeding systems. The magazine sits parallel to the barrel, fitting flush with the top of the receiver, and the ammunition is rotated 90 degrees by a spiral feed ramp before being chambered. The [Heckler & Koch G11](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_G11 "Heckler & Koch G11"), an experimental assault rifle that implements [caseless ammunition](/wiki/Caseless_ammunition "Caseless ammunition"), also functions similarly with the magazine aligned horizontally over the barrel. Rather than being positioned laterally to the barrel like with the aforementioned examples, ammunition is positioned vertically with the bullet facing downward at a 90-degree angle relative to the barrel where it is fed into a rotary chamber before firing. The [AR-57](/wiki/AR-57 "AR-57"), also known as the AR Five-seven, is an upper receiver for the [AR-15](/wiki/AR-15_style_rifle "AR-15 style rifle") rifle lower receiver, firing FN 5.7×28mm rounds from standard FN P90 magazines.

#### Casket

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Casket")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Evers_M4_SMG_mag.svg/250px-Evers_M4_SMG_mag.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Evers_M4_SMG_mag.svg)

Diagram of the Spectre M4 casket magazine

Another form of box magazine, sometimes referred to as a "quad-column", can hold a large amount of ammunition. It is wider than a standard box magazine, but retains the same length. Casket magazines can be found on the [Suomi KP/-31](/wiki/Suomi_KP/-31 "Suomi KP/-31"),[[48]](#cite_note-48) [Hafdasa C-4](/wiki/Hafdasa_C-4 "Hafdasa C-4"), [Spectre M4](/wiki/Spectre_M4 "Spectre M4"), [QCW-05](/wiki/QCW-05 "QCW-05") and on [5.45×39mm](/wiki/5.45%C3%9739mm "5.45×39mm") AK rifle derivatives, and now the Kel-Tec CP33 as well. [Magpul](/wiki/Magpul "Magpul") has been granted a patent[[49]](#cite_note-magpul-pat-49) for a [STANAG](/wiki/STANAG_magazine "STANAG magazine")-compatible casket magazine,[[50]](#cite_note-magpul-50) and such a magazine was also debuted by SureFire in December 2010, and is now sold as the MAG5-60 and MAG5-100 high capacity magazine (HCM) in 60 and 100 round capacities, respectively, in 5.56mm for AR-15 compatible with M4/M16/AR-15 variants and other firearms that accept STANAG 4179 magazines.[[51]](#cite_note-Crane101203-51) [Izhmash](/wiki/Izhmash "Izhmash") has also developed a casket magazine for the [AK-12](/wiki/AK-12 "AK-12").[[50]](#cite_note-magpul-50) Desert Tech have also released the QMAG-53 compatible Quattro-15 lower receiver for the AR-15.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

#### Tandem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Tandem")]

A tandem magazine is a type of box magazine with another magazine placed in front.[[53]](#cite_note-53) When firing, the bolt travels further back past the front section magazine until the rear section is empty, then uses the front section. Firearms using tandem magazines are the [Special Purpose Individual Weapon](/wiki/Special_Purpose_Individual_Weapon "Special Purpose Individual Weapon") (SPIW),[[54]](#cite_note-54) Sunngård pistol, Gerasimenko VAG-73 and the Volkov-Chukhmatov submachine gun. The Mimic Speed9 uses a back-to-back tandem magazine.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/4a/Commons-logo.svg/20px-Commons-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Commons-logo.svg) Media related to [Tandem magazines](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Tandem_magazines "commons:Category:Tandem magazines") at Wikimedia Commons

### Rotary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Rotary")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Ruger_Clip.JPG/250px-Ruger_Clip.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ruger_Clip.JPG)

[Ruger 10/22](/wiki/Ruger_10/22 "Ruger 10/22")'s BX-1CLR rotary magazine

The rotary (or spool) magazine consists of a cylindrical [sprocket](/wiki/Sprocket "Sprocket") actuated by a [torsion spring](/wiki/Torsion_spring "Torsion spring"), with cartridges fitting between the tooth bar of the sprocket, which is mounted on a spindle parallel to the bore axis and rotates each round sequentially into the feeding position. Rotary magazines may be fixed or detachable, and are usually of low capacity, generally 5 to 10 rounds, depending on the caliber used. John Smith patented a rotary magazine in 1856.[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) Another rotary magazine was produced by [Sylvester Roper](/wiki/Sylvester_Roper "Sylvester Roper") in 1866 and was also used in the weapons by Anton Spitalsky and the [Savage Model 1892](/wiki/Savage_Model_99 "Savage Model 99").[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59) Otto Schönauer first patented a spool magazine in 1886[[60]](#cite_note-60) and his later design, patented in 1900,[[61]](#cite_note-61) was used on [bolt-action](/wiki/Bolt-action "Bolt-action") rifles produced at least until 1979,[[62]](#cite_note-62) among them [Mannlicher–Schönauer](/wiki/Mannlicher%E2%80%93Sch%C3%B6nauer "Mannlicher–Schönauer") adopted by the [Greek Army](/wiki/Greek_Army "Greek Army") in 1903. The [M1941 Johnson rifle](/wiki/M1941_Johnson_rifle "M1941 Johnson rifle") and [M1947 Johnson auto carbine](/wiki/M1947_Johnson_auto_carbine "M1947 Johnson auto carbine") uses rotary magazines. The design is still used in some modern firearms, most notably the [Ruger American](/wiki/Ruger_American_Rifle "Ruger American Rifle") series, the semi-automatic [Ruger 10/22](/wiki/Ruger_10/22 "Ruger 10/22"), the bolt-action [Ruger 77/22](/wiki/Ruger_77/22 "Ruger 77/22") and the [Steyr SSG 69](/wiki/Steyr_SSG_69 "Steyr SSG 69").

#### Capsule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Capsule")]

A capsule magazine functions similar to a box magazine, but the spring and follower is stowed away when the magazine bottom is flipped open. The cartridges are loosely dumped into the magazine and spring-fed to the chamber when the bottom is closed.
On the Krag-Jørgensen the magazine is wrapped around the bolt-action to save vertical space and ease loading from the side.

The [Krag-Jorgensen](/wiki/Krag-Jorgensen "Krag-Jorgensen") bolt-action rifle is the only firearm to use this type of magazine and it was adopted by the militaries of Denmark, Norway, and the United States in the late 19th century.

#### Chain/Linkless feed

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Chain/Linkless feed")]

Much like a rotary magazine, this uses an internal continuous chain that works like a conveyor belt. The ARCO Abider, Marek MSG 3J, [Ruger 10/22](/wiki/Ruger_10/22 "Ruger 10/22"),[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64) Sosso 1941 pistol, [Small Arms Ltd. Model 2](/wiki/Sten "Sten"), Moruzh-2 and the Heckler & Koch LMG 11.[[65]](#cite_note-65) uses this magazines.

### Drum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Drum")]

Main article: [Drum magazine](/wiki/Drum_magazine "Drum magazine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Thompson_Magazine.JPG/250px-Thompson_Magazine.JPG)](/wiki/File:Thompson_Magazine.JPG)

50- and 100-round drum magazines plus 20- and 30-round box magazines for Thompson SMG

Drum magazines are used primarily for [light machine guns](/wiki/Light_machine_gun "Light machine gun"). In one type, a moving partition within a cylindrical chamber forces loose rounds into an exit slot, with the cartridges being stored parallel to the axis of rotation. After loading of the magazine, a wound spring or other mechanism forces the partition against the rounds. In all models a single column is pushed by a follower through a curved path. From there the rounds enter the vertical riser either from a single or dual drums. Cylindrical designs such as rotary and drum magazines allow for larger capacity than box magazines, without growing to excessive length. The downside of a drum magazine's extra capacity is its added weight that, combined with the gun, can affect handling and prolonged use. Drum magazines can be more difficult to incorporate into combat gear compared to more regular, rectangular box magazines.

Many drum-fed firearms can also load from conventional box magazines, such as the Soviet [PPSh-41](/wiki/PPSh-41 "PPSh-41") submachine gun, [RPK](/wiki/RPK "RPK") light machine gun, and the American [Thompson submachine gun](/wiki/Thompson_submachine_gun "Thompson submachine gun").

The term "drum" is sometimes applied to a belt box for a belt-fed machine gun, though this is just a case that houses a length of ammunition belt, not a drum magazine.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/4a/Commons-logo.svg/20px-Commons-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Commons-logo.svg) Media related to [Drum magazines](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Drum_magazines "commons:Category:Drum magazines") at Wikimedia Commons

#### Saddle-drum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Saddle-drum")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Double_drum_magazine_filled.svg/250px-Double_drum_magazine_filled.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Double_drum_magazine_filled.svg)

[Beta C-Mag](/wiki/Beta_C-Mag "Beta C-Mag") double-drum magazine

Before WWII the Germans developed 75-round saddle-drum magazines for use in their [MG 13](/wiki/MG_13 "MG 13") and [MG 15](/wiki/MG_15 "MG 15") machine guns. The [MG 34](/wiki/MG_34 "MG 34") machine guns could also use saddle-drum magazine when fitted with a special feed cover. The 75 rounds of ammunition were evenly distributed in each side of the magazine with a central feed "tower" where the ammunition is fed to the bolt. The ammunition was fed by a spring force, with rounds alternating from each side of the double drum so that the gun would not become unbalanced.

### Pan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Pan")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Magazynek_Lewisa_z_polska_amunicja_792mm.jpg/250px-Magazynek_Lewisa_z_polska_amunicja_792mm.jpg)](/wiki/File:Magazynek_Lewisa_z_polska_amunicja_792mm.jpg)

Pan magazine as used on a 7.92x57mm Lewis Gun.

Pan magazines differ from other circular magazines in that the cartridges are stored perpendicular to the axis of rotation, rather than being parallel, and are usually mounted on top of the firearm. This type is used on the [Lewis Gun](/wiki/Lewis_Gun "Lewis Gun"), [Vickers K](/wiki/Vickers_K_machine_gun "Vickers K machine gun"), [Bren Gun](/wiki/Bren_Gun "Bren Gun") (only used in anti-aircraft mountings),[[66]](#cite_note-66) [Degtyaryov light machine gun](/wiki/Degtyaryov_light_machine_gun "Degtyaryov light machine gun"), and [American-180](/wiki/American-180 "American-180") submachine gun. A highly unusual example was found on the [Type 89 machine gun](/wiki/Type_89_machine_gun "Type 89 machine gun") fed from two 45-round quadrant-shaped pan magazines (each magazine held nine of the five-round stripper clips).

### Helical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Helical")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Calico_M960_SMG.jpg/250px-Calico_M960_SMG.jpg)](/wiki/File:Calico_M960_SMG.jpg)

[Calico](/wiki/Calico_Light_Weapons_Systems "Calico Light Weapons Systems") pioneered the helical magazine design. Pictured is the company's [M960](/wiki/Calico_M960 "Calico M960") [carbine](/wiki/Carbine "Carbine").

[Helical](/wiki/Helix "Helix") magazines extend the drum magazine design so that rounds follow a spiral path around an auger-shaped rotating follower or *drive member*, allowing for large ammunition capacity in a relatively compact package (compared to a regular box magazine of similar capacity). Early helical magazine designs include that patented by an unidentified inventor through the patent agent William Edward Newton in 1857 and the integral magazine of the [Evans Repeating Rifle](/wiki/Evans_Repeating_Rifle "Evans Repeating Rifle"), patented in the late 1860s.[[67]](#cite_note-:0-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68) This type of magazine is used by the [Calico M960](/wiki/Calico_M960 "Calico M960"), [PP-19 Bizon](/wiki/PP-19_Bizon "PP-19 Bizon"), [CS/LS06](/wiki/CF-05 "CF-05") and KBP [PP90M1](/wiki/PP-90M1 "PP-90M1"). The North Korean military uses a 100- to 150- round helical magazine in the Type 88 assault rifle.[[69]](#cite_note-69) Helical magazines offer substantially more ammunition carriage; however, they are inherently complex designs. As such, they can be difficult to load and may decrease the reliability of feeding the weapon.[[67]](#cite_note-:0-67)

### Hopper

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Hopper")]

The hopper magazine is a very unusual design. Unlike many other types of magazine-fed machine guns, which commonly used either box magazines or belts to feed ammunition into the firearm's action, the hopper magazine functioned differently. It would use [stripper clips](/wiki/Stripper_clip "Stripper clip") from an infantryman or machine gunner to supply ammunition for the machine gun to operate. This could be accomplished at any time, by just dropping the entire stripper clip into the hopper magazine.

The Japanese [Type 11 light machine gun](/wiki/Type_11_light_machine_gun "Type 11 light machine gun") was the only weapon system that used a hopper magazine. This light machine gun was fed by standard [6.5×50mmSR Arisaka](/wiki/6.5%C3%9750mmSR_Arisaka "6.5×50mmSR Arisaka") stripper clips that were used by riflemen armed with the [Type 38 bolt action rifle](/wiki/Type_38_rifle "Type 38 rifle"). The hopper is located on the left side of the receiver and held 6 of the 5-round clips, for a total of 30 rounds of ammunition. The hopper magazine was designed with a series of mechanical teeth activated by a cam track on the gas piston to pull cartridges off each clip and into the action. After the fifth and final round from each stripper clip was fed and fired, the empty clip would then fall out the bottom of the hopper magazine and the next fully loaded stripper clip would then be dropped into place for feeding. There is a spring-loaded follower that applied pressure on top of the clips to hold them in place so they would not fall out while the weapon was being transported or fired.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

## STANAG magazine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: STANAG magazine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Stanag_mags.jpg/250px-Stanag_mags.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stanag_mags.jpg)

Two STANAG-compliant magazines: A 20-round Colt-manufactured magazine, and a 30-round Heckler & Koch "high reliability" magazine.

Main article: [STANAG magazine](/wiki/STANAG_magazine "STANAG magazine")

A *STANAG magazine*[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72) or *NATO magazine* is a type of detachable magazine proposed by [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") in October 1980.[[73]](#cite_note-73) Shortly after NATO's acceptance of the [5.56×45mm NATO](/wiki/5.56%C3%9745mm_NATO "5.56×45mm NATO") rifle cartridge, Draft Standardization Agreement ([STANAG](/wiki/STANAG "STANAG")) 4179 was proposed in order to allow NATO members to easily share rifle ammunition and magazines down to the individual soldier level. The U.S. M16 rifle magazine was proposed for standardization. Many NATO members subsequently developed or purchased rifles with the ability to accept this type of magazine. However, the standard was never ratified and remains a "Draft STANAG".[[74]](#cite_note-74)

The STANAG magazine concept is only an interface, dimensional, and control (magazine latch, bolt stop, etc.) requirement.[[75]](#cite_note-books.google.com-75)[[76]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-76) Therefore, it not only allows one type of magazine to interface with various weapon systems,[[75]](#cite_note-books.google.com-75)[[76]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-76) but also allows STANAG magazines to be made in various configurations and capacities.[[75]](#cite_note-books.google.com-75)[[76]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-76) The standard STANAG magazines are 20, 30, and 40 round box magazines,[[76]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-76) but there are many other designs available with capacities ranging from one round[[77]](#cite_note-77) to 60 and 100 round casket magazines,[[50]](#cite_note-magpul-50)[[78]](#cite_note-78) 90 round [snail-drum magazines](/wiki/Drum_magazine "Drum magazine"),[[79]](#cite_note-79) and 100 round[[80]](#cite_note-80) and 150 round double-drum magazines.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

## High-capacity magazine bans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: High-capacity magazine bans")]

A high-capacity magazine ban is a law which bans or otherwise restricts detachable [firearm magazines](/wiki/Firearm_magazine "Firearm magazine") that can hold more than a certain number of [rounds](/wiki/Cartridge_(firearms) "Cartridge (firearms)") of [ammunition](/wiki/Ammunition "Ammunition"). The concept of a high-capacity magazine (or large-capacity magazine) has no general definition as "standard" capacities may vary. The term has meaning only in reference to specific laws in specific jurisdictions. In this legal sense, a magazine may be defined as high-capacity based on the number of rounds that are allowed by law in a particular jurisdiction.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

In the United States, a number of states have passed laws that ban magazines which are defined as "high-capacity" by statute.[[83]](#cite_note-LCPGVLargeCap-83) High-capacity or large-capacity magazines are generally those defined by statute to be capable of holding more than 10 to 15 rounds, although the definitions will vary by state. The now expired [Federal Assault Weapons Ban of 1994](/wiki/Federal_Assault_Weapons_Ban "Federal Assault Weapons Ban") restricted magazines that could hold more than ten cartridges.[[83]](#cite_note-LCPGVLargeCap-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85) Other nations impose restrictions on magazine capacity as well. In Canada, magazines are generally limited to 5 rounds for [rifles](/wiki/Rifle "Rifle") and [shotguns](/wiki/Shotgun "Shotgun") (with some exceptions) and 10 rounds for [handguns](/wiki/Handgun "Handgun") (with some exceptions), depending on the firearm.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

### By country

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: By country")]

#### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Australia")]

Main article: [Gun laws of Australia](/wiki/Gun_laws_of_Australia "Gun laws of Australia")

In Australia, handgun, [semi-automatic rimfire](/wiki/Semi-automatic_rifle "Semi-automatic rifle"), [pump-action](/wiki/Pump-action "Pump-action") and [lever-action](/wiki/Lever-action "Lever-action") rifle magazines (excluding tubular magazines like those found in [gallery guns](/wiki/Gallery_gun "Gallery gun") or [Winchester rifles](/wiki/Winchester_rifles "Winchester rifles") for manually repeating rifles) holding more than ten rounds, [shotgun](/wiki/Shotgun "Shotgun") magazines holding more than five rounds, and [bolt-action](/wiki/Bolt-action "Bolt-action") and [straight-pull](/wiki/Straight-pull "Straight-pull") rifle magazines holding more than 15 rounds are heavily restricted (Category D).[[87]](#cite_note-87)

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Canada")]

Main article: [Gun laws in Canada](/wiki/Gun_laws_in_Canada "Gun laws in Canada")

In Canada, magazines designed for use in [semi-automatic centrefire rifles](/wiki/Semi-auto_rifle "Semi-auto rifle") and semi-automatic shotguns are limited to 5 rounds, and magazines designed for use in handguns are limited to 10 rounds. Magazines designed for use in semi-automatic rimfire rifles, as well as manually operated long guns, are exempt from the magazine capacity restrictions.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

#### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Russia")]

In [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), all magazines for use with any type of firearm except sport handguns and award weapons are limited to no more than 10 rounds.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

#### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

There are no capacity restrictions on detachable magazines in the United Kingdom. However, since January 1989, any shotgun with a detachable magazine, or a non-detachable magazine capable of holding more than two cartridges is classed as a Section 1 firearm and must be held on a firearm certificate, which is subject to more stringent requirements than "normal" section 2 shotguns held on a shotgun certificate.[[90]](#cite_note-90) Section 2 shotguns include break-barrel guns with no magazine, as well as repeating and semi-automatic guns with fixed two-round magazines. When the 1988 Act was introduced, many guns with larger (often [tubular magazines](#Tubular)) were brought into compliance by crimping the magazine.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: United States")]

##### U.S. Federal law

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: U.S. Federal law")]

Between 1994 and 2004, the [Federal Assault Weapons Ban](/wiki/Federal_Assault_Weapons_Ban "Federal Assault Weapons Ban"), which included a ban on high-capacity magazines, was in effect. It prohibited new magazines over 10 rounds in the United States.[[92]](#cite_note-KeslingElinson2-92) After the expiration of the ban, there is no nationwide prohibition against the possession of high-capacity magazines, which are considered an unregulated firearm accessory.[[92]](#cite_note-KeslingElinson2-92)

Legislation to restore a federal high-capacity magazine ban has been repeatedly introduced by [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") in the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") since the expiration of the Federal Assault Weapons Ban, especially in the wake of [mass shootings in the United States](/wiki/Mass_shootings_in_the_United_States "Mass shootings in the United States") in which high-capacity magazines were used, including the [Tucson shooting](/wiki/2011_Tucson_shooting "2011 Tucson shooting") (2011), the [Aurora, Colorado movie theater shooting](/wiki/2012_Aurora,_Colorado_shooting "2012 Aurora, Colorado shooting") (2012), and the [Harvest music festival shooting](/wiki/2017_Las_Vegas_shooting "2017 Las Vegas shooting") (2017).[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94) These efforts have been thus far unsuccessful. The federal Keep Americans Safe Act, which would restore the ban on new magazines that hold more than 10 rounds, passed the [House Judiciary Committee](/wiki/House_Judiciary_Committee "House Judiciary Committee") in September 2019.[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) The D.C. Court of Appeals in its *[Benson v. United States](/w/index.php?title=Benson_v._United_States&action=edit&redlink=1 "Benson v. United States (page does not exist)")* decision struck down magazine bans, ruling that laws limiting the capacity of magazines are unconstitutional, in March 2026.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

##### State laws

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Magazine_(firearms)&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: State laws")]

A number of U.S. states as well as [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") have high-capacity magazine restrictions or bans.[[98]](#cite_note-LCPGV-LCMPolSum-98)

* [California](/wiki/California "California") (on in-state sales of magazines with a capacity of more than 10 rounds)
* [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado") (on in-state sales of magazines with a capacity of more than 15 rounds)
* [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut")
* [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware") (17 rounds)
* [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii") (on handguns only)
* [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") (10 rounds for rifles or shotguns, 15 rounds for handguns)[[99]](#cite_note-PICA-Tribune-99)
* [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland") (on in-state sales of magazines with a capacity of more than 10 rounds)
* [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts")
* [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey")
* [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)")
* [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon")
* [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island")
* [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont") (10-round magazine limit on rifles and a 15-round limit on handguns)
* [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)") (on sale, manufacture, and importation of magazines with a capacity of more than 10 rounds, but possession is legal)[[100]](#cite_note-100)

The types of acts prohibited vary among the jurisdictions; most prohibit manufacturer, sale, or possession, but some states' laws are narrower (Maryland law does not ban possession of high-capacity magazines) while other states' laws are broader (some states also ban the transfer, transportation, or acquisition of high-capacity magazines).[[101]](#cite_note-GiffordsCount-101) Some states' laws include "grandfather" pre-ban high-capacity magazines, exempting these from their law, while other states' laws do not.[[101]](#cite_note-GiffordsCount-101)
